speaker: OCTAVIUS
text: Now, Antony, our hopes are answerèd.

speaker: OCTAVIUS
text: You said the enemy would not come down

speaker: OCTAVIUS
text: But keep the hills and upper regions.

speaker: OCTAVIUS
text: It proves not so; their battles are at hand.

speaker: OCTAVIUS
text: They mean to warn us at Philippi here,

speaker: OCTAVIUS
text: Answering before we do demand of them.

speaker: ANTONY
text: Tut, I am in their bosoms, and I know

speaker: ANTONY
text: Wherefore they do it. They could be content

speaker: ANTONY
text: To visit other places, and come down

speaker: ANTONY
text: With fearful bravery, thinking by this face

speaker: ANTONY
text: To fasten in our thoughts that they have courage.

speaker: ANTONY
text: But ’tis not so.

speaker: MESSENGER
text: Prepare you, generals.

speaker: MESSENGER
text: The enemy comes on in gallant show.

speaker: MESSENGER
text: Their bloody sign of battle is hung out,

speaker: MESSENGER
text: And something to be done immediately.

speaker: ANTONY
text: Octavius, lead your battle softly on

speaker: ANTONY
text: Upon the left hand of the even field.

speaker: OCTAVIUS
text: Upon the right hand, I; keep thou the left.

speaker: ANTONY
text: Why do you cross me in this exigent?

speaker: OCTAVIUS
text: I do not cross you, but I will do so.

speaker: BRUTUS
text: They stand and would have parley.

speaker: CASSIUS
text: Stand fast, Titinius. We must out and talk.

speaker: OCTAVIUS
text: Mark Antony, shall we give sign of battle?

speaker: ANTONY
text: No, Caesar, we will answer on their charge.

speaker: ANTONY
text: Make forth. The Generals would have some words.

speaker: OCTAVIUS
text: Stir not until the signal.

speaker: BRUTUS
text: Words before blows; is it so, countrymen?

speaker: OCTAVIUS
text: Not that we love words better, as you do.

speaker: BRUTUS
text: Good words are better than bad strokes, Octavius.

speaker: ANTONY
text: In your bad strokes, Brutus, you give good words.

speaker: ANTONY
text: Witness the hole you made in Caesar’s heart,

speaker: ANTONY
text: Crying “Long live, hail, Caesar!”

speaker: CASSIUS
text: Antony,

speaker: CASSIUS
text: The posture of your blows are yet unknown,

speaker: CASSIUS
text: But, for your words, they rob the Hybla bees

speaker: CASSIUS
text: And leave them honeyless.

speaker: ANTONY
text: Not stingless too.

speaker: BRUTUS
text: O yes, and soundless too,

speaker: BRUTUS
text: For you have stolen their buzzing, Antony,

speaker: BRUTUS
text: And very wisely threat before you sting.

speaker: ANTONY
text: Villains, you did not so when your vile daggers

speaker: ANTONY
text: Hacked one another in the sides of Caesar.

speaker: ANTONY
text: You showed your teeth like apes and fawned like hounds

speaker: ANTONY
text: And bowed like bondmen, kissing Caesar’s feet,

speaker: ANTONY
text: Whilst damnèd Casca, like a cur, behind

speaker: ANTONY
text: Struck Caesar on the neck. O you flatterers!

speaker: CASSIUS
text: Flatterers?—Now, Brutus, thank yourself!

speaker: CASSIUS
text: This tongue had not offended so today

speaker: CASSIUS
text: If Cassius might have ruled.

speaker: OCTAVIUS
text: Come, come, the cause. If arguing make us sweat,

speaker: OCTAVIUS
text: The proof of it will turn to redder drops.

speaker: OCTAVIUS
text: Look, I draw a sword against conspirators;

speaker: OCTAVIUS
text: When think you that the sword goes up again?

speaker: OCTAVIUS
text: Never, till Caesar’s three and thirty wounds

speaker: OCTAVIUS
text: Be well avenged, or till another Caesar

speaker: OCTAVIUS
text: Have added slaughter to the sword of traitors.

speaker: BRUTUS
text: Caesar, thou canst not die by traitors’ hands

speaker: BRUTUS
text: Unless thou bring’st them with thee.

speaker: OCTAVIUS
text: So I hope.

speaker: OCTAVIUS
text: I was not born to die on Brutus’ sword.

speaker: BRUTUS
text: O, if thou wert the noblest of thy strain,

speaker: BRUTUS
text: Young man, thou couldst not die more honorable.

speaker: CASSIUS
text: A peevish schoolboy, worthless of such honor,

speaker: CASSIUS
text: Joined with a masker and a reveler!

speaker: ANTONY
text: Old Cassius still.

speaker: OCTAVIUS
text: Come, Antony, away!—

speaker: OCTAVIUS
text: Defiance, traitors, hurl we in your teeth.

speaker: OCTAVIUS
text: If you dare fight today, come to the field;

speaker: OCTAVIUS
text: If not, when you have stomachs.

speaker: CASSIUS
text: Why now, blow wind, swell billow, and swim bark!

speaker: CASSIUS
text: The storm is up, and all is on the hazard.

speaker: BRUTUS
text: Ho, Lucilius, hark, a word with you.

speaker: LUCILIUS
text: My lord?

speaker: CASSIUS
text: Messala.

speaker: MESSALA
text: What says my general?

speaker: CASSIUS
text: Messala,

speaker: CASSIUS
text: This is my birthday, as this very day

speaker: CASSIUS
text: Was Cassius born. Give me thy hand, Messala.

speaker: CASSIUS
text: Be thou my witness that against my will

speaker: CASSIUS
text: (As Pompey was) am I compelled to set

speaker: CASSIUS
text: Upon one battle all our liberties.

speaker: CASSIUS
text: You know that I held Epicurus strong

speaker: CASSIUS
text: And his opinion. Now I change my mind

speaker: CASSIUS
text: And partly credit things that do presage.

speaker: CASSIUS
text: Coming from Sardis, on our former ensign

speaker: CASSIUS
text: Two mighty eagles fell, and there they perched,

speaker: CASSIUS
text: Gorging and feeding from our soldiers’ hands,

speaker: CASSIUS
text: Who to Philippi here consorted us.

speaker: CASSIUS
text: This morning are they fled away and gone,

speaker: CASSIUS
text: And in their steads do ravens, crows, and kites

speaker: CASSIUS
text: Fly o’er our heads and downward look on us

speaker: CASSIUS
text: As we were sickly prey. Their shadows seem

speaker: CASSIUS
text: A canopy most fatal, under which

speaker: CASSIUS
text: Our army lies, ready to give up the ghost.

speaker: MESSALA
text: Believe not so.

speaker: CASSIUS
text: I but believe it partly,

speaker: CASSIUS
text: For I am fresh of spirit and resolved

speaker: CASSIUS
text: To meet all perils very constantly.

speaker: BRUTUS
text: Even so, Lucilius.

speaker: CASSIUS
text: Now, most noble Brutus,

speaker: CASSIUS
text: The gods today stand friendly that we may,

speaker: CASSIUS
text: Lovers in peace, lead on our days to age.

speaker: CASSIUS
text: But since the affairs of men rests still incertain,

speaker: CASSIUS
text: Let’s reason with the worst that may befall.

speaker: CASSIUS
text: If we do lose this battle, then is this

speaker: CASSIUS
text: The very last time we shall speak together.

speaker: CASSIUS
text: What are you then determinèd to do?

speaker: BRUTUS
text: Even by the rule of that philosophy

speaker: BRUTUS
text: By which I did blame Cato for the death

speaker: BRUTUS
text: Which he did give himself (I know not how,

speaker: BRUTUS
text: But I do find it cowardly and vile,

speaker: BRUTUS
text: For fear of what might fall, so to prevent

speaker: BRUTUS
text: The time of life), arming myself with patience

speaker: BRUTUS
text: To stay the providence of some high powers

speaker: BRUTUS
text: That govern us below.

speaker: CASSIUS
text: Then, if we lose this battle,

speaker: CASSIUS
text: You are contented to be led in triumph

speaker: CASSIUS
text: Thorough the streets of Rome?

speaker: BRUTUS
text: No, Cassius, no. Think not, thou noble Roman,

speaker: BRUTUS
text: That ever Brutus will go bound to Rome.

speaker: BRUTUS
text: He bears too great a mind. But this same day

speaker: BRUTUS
text: Must end that work the ides of March begun.

speaker: BRUTUS
text: And whether we shall meet again, I know not.

speaker: BRUTUS
text: Therefore our everlasting farewell take.

speaker: BRUTUS
text: Forever and forever farewell, Cassius.

speaker: BRUTUS
text: If we do meet again, why we shall smile;

speaker: BRUTUS
text: If not, why then this parting was well made.

speaker: CASSIUS
text: Forever and forever farewell, Brutus.

speaker: CASSIUS
text: If we do meet again, we’ll smile indeed;

speaker: CASSIUS
text: If not, ’tis true this parting was well made.

speaker: BRUTUS
text: Why then, lead on.—O, that a man might know

speaker: BRUTUS
text: The end of this day’s business ere it come!

speaker: BRUTUS
text: But it sufficeth that the day will end,

speaker: BRUTUS
text: And then the end is known.—Come ho, away!
